stem: Why must an abstract quantum circuit be transpiled before it can be run on IBM Quantum hardware through the Runtime primitives?

options.text: Hardware executes only ISA circuits: gates must be expressed in the device's native basis, and every two-qubit gate must act on physically-connected qubits — transpilation rewrites and routes the circuit to satisfy both.
Transpilation is optional; the V2 primitives silently convert any abstract circuit at submission time, so it only saves a little runtime.
Transpilation is needed only to draw the circuit nicely; execution accepts any circuit unchanged.
Transpilation exists to convert the circuit into OpenQASM 2, the only format the hardware accepts.

explanation.correct: Real devices have a fixed native gate set and a limited qubit connectivity. Transpilation maps virtual qubits to physical ones, routes two-qubit gates onto connected pairs (inserting SWAPs), and translates gates into the basis set — producing an ISA circuit, which is what the V2 primitives require as input.

distractors.B: The V2 primitives do NOT silently transpile arbitrary circuits; they expect ISA circuits and will error on non-ISA input. Transpiling is a required step, not a mere optimization.
distractors.C: Drawing has nothing to do with it; an un-transpiled circuit generally cannot execute on hardware at all.
distractors.D: Hardware execution is not about OpenQASM 2; the requirement is native basis gates plus valid connectivity (an ISA circuit).
